title: Finding the lock that THC fits

study.citation: Lisa A. Matsuda, Stephen J. Lolait, Michael J. Brownstein, Alice C. Young & Tom I. Bonner (1990), "Structure of a cannabinoid receptor and functional expression of the cloned cDNA," Nature 346(6284), 561-564

tldr: Researchers at the U.S. National Institutes of Health found and copied the gene for a brain receptor that THC plugs into — the first hard proof the body comes built with a docking site for cannabis, and the discovery that opened up the whole field of cannabinoid biology.

sections.body: By 1990 it was obvious that the active chemicals in cannabis — THC chief among them — did powerful things to the brain: they shifted mood, memory, appetite, and pain. The puzzle was how. A drug only works by latching onto something. Earlier studies had hinted that THC sticks to a specific spot on brain cells, called a receptor — a kind of molecular lock that a chemical key fits into to send a signal inside the cell. But nobody had actually isolated that lock. The team wanted to find the gene that builds it, copy that gene, and prove the protein it makes really responds to cannabis chemicals.

They went hunting through rat brain tissue for the gene's recipe. Genes are written in DNA, and a working copy of a gene is a stretch of code called a cDNA. The researchers fished out a candidate cDNA that looked like it belonged to a known family of receptors — the kind that pass a message into a cell by switching on a partner molecule called a G protein. To test it, they took that gene copy and inserted it into cultured cells that normally have no cannabinoid receptors at all. If those cells suddenly started reacting to cannabis chemicals, the gene had to be the real thing.

It worked. The engineered cells now responded to cannabinoids by turning down an enzyme called adenylate cyclase, which the cell uses to make an internal messenger. The response was dose-dependent — more drug, bigger effect — and stereoselective, meaning the receptor told apart mirror-image versions of a molecule and reacted to the right-handed one. It also matched the chemistry of cannabis: compounds that get you high switched the receptor on, while inactive look-alikes did little. And the gene's activity showed up in exactly the brain regions already known to bind cannabinoids. Everything lined up.

This is the study that turned cannabis from folklore into molecular biology. Once you have the receptor's gene, you can ask the next questions: if the body builds a lock for THC, it surely makes its own keys — and within two years researchers found one, a natural brain chemical named anandamide. That receptor, now called CB1, plus the body's own cannabinoid signals became known as the endocannabinoid system, a control network for pain, appetite, mood, and memory. Every modern cannabis drug, and a generation of research into how the brain regulates itself, traces back to this single cloned gene.

The work was done in rats and in cultured cells, so it mapped the machinery without telling you what any of it feels like or does in a living person. It also found only the first receptor; a second major one, CB2, common in immune tissue, was identified a few years later, and the body's own cannabinoid chemicals were still unknown when this paper came out. Knowing the lock exists is a starting point — it does not by itself explain why cannabis helps some conditions, harms in others, or behaves so differently from one person to the next.

terms.def: A protein on a cell that acts like a lock: when the right chemical key fits into it, it triggers a signal inside the cell. THC works by fitting this particular lock.
A clean, working copy of a gene's code that scientists can move into other cells. Inserting it lets a cell build the protein that gene describes — here, the cannabinoid receptor.
The body's own network of cannabinoid receptors and the natural chemicals that activate them, helping regulate things like pain, appetite, mood, and memory. This study found its first receptor.

takeaways: Cannabis affects the brain because the brain comes pre-built with a receptor — a molecular lock — that THC happens to fit; this study was the first to isolate the gene for it.
The proof was clean: put the gene into cells that had no such receptor, and those cells started responding to cannabinoids in a dose-dependent, chemistry-matching way.
Finding the lock implied the body makes its own keys — leading to the discovery of anandamide and the whole endocannabinoid system that drug research builds on today.

quiz.q: What did the researchers actually isolate and copy in this study?
choices: A new strain of cannabis plant
The gene (cDNA) for a brain receptor that responds to cannabinoids
A natural chemical the body makes
quiz.why: They cloned the cDNA — a working copy of the gene — for the cannabinoid receptor. That gave them the recipe for the 'lock' that THC fits, which is what made everything that followed possible.

quiz.q: How did they prove the gene they found was really the cannabinoid receptor?
choices: They gave cannabis to volunteers and watched for a high
They inserted the gene into cells with no such receptor and showed those cells then responded to cannabinoids
They found it only in cannabis plants
They measured how much people smoked
quiz.why: Functional expression: put the cloned gene into cells that normally ignore cannabinoids. When those cells suddenly reacted — in a dose-dependent, stereoselective, chemistry-matching way — the gene had to encode the real receptor.

quiz.q: Why was finding this receptor such a big deal for the field?
choices: It implied the body makes its own cannabinoid-like chemicals, opening up the endocannabinoid system
It replaced all cannabis research with computer models
It showed THC has no effect on the brain
quiz.why: If the body builds a lock, it likely makes its own keys. That logic led to the discovery of anandamide and the mapping of the endocannabinoid system — a control network behind pain, appetite, mood, and memory.
